faqs.a: Sensory overload is when input from the senses outpaces the brain's ability to sort and process it. The usual filter that screens out background input struggles, so sights, sounds, and textures all compete at once and become overwhelming.

It's especially common in autistic people and people with ADHD, whose sensory processing often differs. It also shows up with anxiety, PTSD, migraines, and during periods of stress or exhaustion, so it isn't unique to any one condition.

faqs.q: How do people cope with sensory overload?
faqs.a: Many people reduce input by getting to a quieter, dimmer space, covering the ears or eyes, and taking time alone to recover afterward. If overload happens often or interferes with daily life, a clinician can suggest accommodations and coping strategies.
